questions.question_text: A buyer's attorney is not present at closing in Indiana. Is the closing still legally valid?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, attorney attendance at closing is optional in Indiana
distractors: No, Indiana requires an attorney to oversee all residential closings
No, an attorney must at least review all documents before closing
Yes, but only for transactions under $500,000
questions.explanation: Indiana is an attorney-optional state for real estate closings. Title companies typically conduct closings without attorney involvement, which is legally valid. Buyers and sellers may choose to hire attorneys, but it is not required by Indiana law.

questions.question_text: Which entity typically conducts real estate closings in Indiana?
questions.correct_answer: Title companies are the standard closing agents
distractors: An attorney is legally required to conduct the closing
The county recorder's office oversees each closing
IREC must approve all residential closings
questions.explanation: Indiana is an attorney-optional state, meaning closings are typically conducted by title companies rather than attorneys. While buyers and sellers may hire attorneys, it is not required by law. The county recorder records the deed but does not conduct closings.

questions.question_text: A buyer is budgeting for closing costs in Indiana. Should the buyer include a state real estate transfer tax in the estimate?
questions.correct_answer: No, Indiana does not impose a state real estate transfer tax
distractors: Yes, Indiana charges 0.1% of the sale price as a transfer tax
Yes, the transfer tax applies to sales over $300,000
Yes, but sellers pay the tax and it does not affect buyer costs
questions.explanation: Indiana does not have a state real estate transfer tax, which distinguishes it from many other states. Buyers and sellers should budget for other closing costs — title insurance, recording fees, lender fees — but not a state transfer tax.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks whether they need title insurance for their Indiana purchase. The broker explains that title insurance is the standard method of title protection in Indiana. Why is this important?
questions.correct_answer: Title insurance protects against defects in the title not revealed by a title search
distractors: Title insurance is required by Indiana law for all residential purchases
Title insurance replaces the need for a deed to be recorded
Title insurance guarantees the property's appraised value for 10 years
questions.explanation: Title insurance is the standard method of title protection in Indiana because it covers defects — such as undisclosed liens, forgeries, or errors in public records — that a title search might not reveal. While not legally required in all cases, lenders typically require it, and it protects the buyer's ownership interest.

questions.question_text: A buyer discovers three weeks after closing that the title company failed to identify an unpaid mechanic's lien on the property. The buyer has an owner's title insurance policy. What protection does the buyer have?
questions.correct_answer: The title insurance policy covers losses from defects that existed before the policy date
distractors: No protection; the buyer should have ordered an independent title search
Coverage only if the buyer can prove the title company was negligent
Coverage only for defects discovered within 30 days of closing
questions.explanation: An owner's title insurance policy protects against pre-existing defects in title, including liens that existed before the policy was issued. The policy continues to protect the buyer regardless of when the defect is discovered, as long as it predated the policy. The buyer does not need to prove negligence to make a claim.

questions.question_text: A broker collects a $5,000 earnest money check from a buyer and places it in the firm's operating account pending the managing broker's review. What has the broker done wrong?
questions.correct_answer: Commingled client funds with the firm's operating account
distractors: Nothing; funds may be held briefly in operating accounts during review
Converted client funds by placing them in a non-client account
Violated escrow rules by accepting a check rather than wire transfer
questions.explanation: Earnest money must be deposited into the trust account (or delivered to the party designated in the purchase agreement) within two banking days after acceptance (876 IAC 8-2-2(a)). Placing client funds in the operating account — even briefly, pending review — is commingling (IC 25-34.1-4-5).

questions.question_text: A managing broker reconciles the trust account and discovers $800 more than expected. What should the managing broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Investigate the source and correct the records or notify affected parties
distractors: Transfer the overage to operating funds as unclaimed income
No action needed; overages are not a violation
Report the overage to IREC within 24 hours as a trust surplus
questions.explanation: An unexplained overage in a trust account indicates a bookkeeping error or misapplied funds and requires investigation. The funds may belong to a client, and transferring them to operating funds without identification would constitute conversion. The managing broker must correct the records and ensure client funds are properly accounted for.

questions.question_text: A broker disburses earnest money to the seller before the closing agent has confirmed that all closing conditions are satisfied. Is this practice appropriate?
questions.correct_answer: No, disbursement should follow confirmation that all closing conditions are met
distractors: Yes, disbursing on the scheduled closing date is standard practice
Yes, if both parties verbally confirm they are ready to close
Yes, provided the amount is under the FDIC insurance limit
questions.explanation: Proper handling of client funds requires that disbursements follow the satisfaction of all closing conditions — signed documents, funds received, lender conditions met. Disbursing prematurely exposes the broker to liability if the transaction does not complete.

questions.question_text: A buyer demands a receipt for the earnest money check immediately upon delivery to the broker. Is the buyer entitled to this?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, brokers must provide documentation and receipts for all client funds received
distractors: No, receipts are only provided upon request after trust account deposit
No, the purchase agreement serves as sufficient documentation
Yes, but only if the check exceeds $1,000
questions.explanation: Proper accounting of client funds requires documentation and receipts. A buyer is entitled to a receipt confirming the broker received the earnest money. This is part of the broker's duty to account for all client funds, regardless of check amount or whether the purchase agreement is already signed.

questions.question_text: A transaction fails to close due to a financing contingency. The purchase agreement specifies the earnest money is refundable if financing is not obtained. The seller refuses to sign a release. What should the broker do with the earnest money?
questions.correct_answer: Retain the funds and seek written mutual authorization or court direction before disbursing
distractors: Refund to the buyer immediately since the agreement specifies refundability
Release to the seller as the principal party to the transaction
Donate the funds to an escrow reserve if no resolution is reached in 60 days
questions.explanation: Even when the purchase agreement specifies that earnest money is refundable, the broker cannot disburse unilaterally if the seller refuses to authorize release. Without mutual written authorization or a court order, the broker must hold the funds to avoid liability. Releasing to the buyer without seller consent could expose the broker to a claim from the seller. Indiana also allows the broker to send all parties certified-mail notice of intended disbursement; if no mutual release is signed and no lawsuit is filed within 60 days, the broker may disburse as noticed (876 IAC 8-2-2(d)).

questions.question_text: A managing broker has 12 brokers working in the firm. Which of the following best describes the managing broker's supervisory obligation?
questions.correct_answer: General supervision of all firm brokers and enforcement of legal compliance
distractors: Supervision only of brokers who handle trust account transactions
Personal review of every transaction document before closing
Supervision only of newly licensed brokers in their first year
questions.explanation: Indiana managing brokers are responsible for general supervision of all brokers in the firm — not just those handling trust accounts or new licensees. This includes enforcing compliance with Indiana law and Commission rules across all firm activities. Personal review of every document is not required, but adequate oversight is.

questions.question_text: A broker in the firm runs an advertisement that violates IREC advertising rules. The managing broker was unaware of the ad. Who is held responsible by IREC?
questions.correct_answer: The managing broker bears responsibility for all firm advertising compliance
distractors: Only the broker who placed the advertisement
The brokerage entity is fined but the managing broker faces no personal action
No one, because the managing broker did not approve the advertisement
questions.explanation: Advertising oversight is among the managing broker's supervisory responsibilities. IREC holds managing brokers accountable for all advertising conducted by brokers in their firm. Not personally approving an advertisement does not insulate the managing broker from regulatory responsibility.

questions.question_text: A managing broker has no written training program for new brokers at the firm. An untrained broker makes a significant disclosure error on a transaction. What is the managing broker's exposure?
questions.correct_answer: Potential discipline for inadequate supervision and failure to train
distractors: No exposure; training is optional under Indiana law
Exposure only if the managing broker personally knew of the error
Exposure only if the client files a complaint within 30 days
questions.explanation: The broker company and managing broker are responsible for the actions of their associated brokers and for directing the company's business (IC 25-34.1-4-1, IC 25-34.1-4-2; 876 IAC 8-1-1), so an associated broker's disclosure error can expose the managing broker to discipline for inadequate supervision regardless of personal knowledge or whether a complaint is filed. A written training program is risk-management best practice rather than a statutory requirement, but its absence makes a supervision failure harder to defend.

questions.question_text: A managing broker wants to delegate all trust account oversight to a senior broker in the firm. Under Indiana law, can the managing broker fully delegate this responsibility?
questions.correct_answer: No, the managing broker retains personal responsibility for trust account management
distractors: Yes, with written authorization from IREC to designate a trust account manager
Yes, provided the designated broker holds a managing broker license
Yes, if the firm's written policy authorizes the delegation
questions.explanation: The managing broker's personal responsibility for trust account management cannot be fully delegated under Indiana law. While a senior broker may assist with daily tasks, the managing broker remains personally accountable for trust account compliance. IREC looks to the managing broker in all trust account matters.

questions.question_text: IREC initiates an investigation into a firm's practices. The managing broker is cooperative but is out of the country for three months. Which action best demonstrates the managing broker's ongoing supervisory responsibility?
questions.correct_answer: Designate a qualified broker to respond on behalf of the firm and stay informed
distractors: Pause the investigation until the managing broker returns
Ask IREC to postpone all actions pending the managing broker's availability
Assign another broker to manage the investigation without any oversight
questions.explanation: Supervisory responsibilities do not pause when the managing broker is unavailable. The managing broker must make arrangements to ensure the firm remains compliant and responsive to regulatory inquiries at all times. Designating a qualified proxy while staying informed reflects proper management of supervisory duties.

questions.question_text: A broker in Indiana is classified as an independent contractor for federal tax purposes. Who sets the terms of the commission split between the broker and the firm?
questions.correct_answer: The broker and the firm by private written agreement
distractors: IREC, which sets minimum commission splits by rule
The Indiana Department of Revenue based on income levels
The MLS, which establishes standard split percentages
questions.explanation: Commission splits between brokers and their firms are private contractual arrangements — not regulated by IREC, the MLS, or any government agency. Independent contractor status means the broker negotiates compensation terms directly with the firm.

questions.question_text: A buyer's broker from Firm A closes a transaction with the listing broker from Firm B. At closing, the seller wants to hand the buyer's individual broker a personal commission check, bypassing Firm A entirely. Is this permissible in Indiana?
questions.correct_answer: No, all commissions must flow through the managing broker and firm
distractors: Yes, the seller may pay any party directly with written consent
Yes, if the buyer's broker's firm provides written authorization
No, unless both brokers are independently licensed as managing brokers
questions.explanation: Compensation for an associated broker's licensed activity is paid to the broker's company, which pays the broker under their private split agreement; a fee may never be paid directly to a broker associated with another firm (876 IAC 8-1-9). A seller may agree to compensate the buyer's brokerage, but the payment runs to the firm, not the individual licensee.

questions.question_text: Firm A and Firm B agree to cooperate on a transaction. Firm B brings the buyer and is to receive a portion of the commission. Who pays Firm B's cooperating commission?
questions.correct_answer: Firm A pays Firm B's managing broker as a firm-to-firm transaction
distractors: The seller pays Firm B directly as compensation for services
The buyer pays Firm B directly per the buyer agency agreement
Firm A pays the individual cooperating broker at Firm B directly
questions.explanation: Commission sharing in Indiana occurs between firms — from Firm A's managing broker to Firm B's managing broker — not between individual brokers. An individual broker at Firm B receives their share from their own firm's internal split arrangement. Direct payment from one firm to an individual broker at another firm is not permitted.

questions.question_text: Firm A lists a property and offers 2.5% cooperation to the buyer's firm. A broker from Firm B brings a buyer and the transaction closes. Firm B's broker demands the cooperation fee be paid directly to him rather than to Firm B. What must happen?
questions.correct_answer: The fee must be paid to Firm B; Firm B then pays its broker
distractors: Firm A may pay the individual broker directly with managing broker approval
The fee may be split half to Firm B and half directly to the broker
The seller can direct the fee to the individual broker as a bonus
questions.explanation: Indiana requires commission payments between firms, not to individual brokers outside their own firm. The cooperation fee flows from Firm A to Firm B, and Firm B pays its broker according to their private compensation agreement. No direct payment from Firm A to Firm B's individual broker is permissible.

questions.question_text: Two cooperating firms have only a verbal cooperation agreement on a listed property. The transaction closes but a dispute arises about the commission split. What is the problem with the verbal agreement?
questions.correct_answer: Verbal cooperation agreements are difficult to enforce; written agreements are strongly recommended
distractors: Verbal agreements are void and unenforceable under Indiana law
The MLS offer of cooperation automatically creates a binding written agreement
Indiana requires the seller to approve all cooperation agreements in writing
questions.explanation: MLSs no longer carry offers of compensation (since August 2024), so inter-firm compensation should be set out in a separate written agreement. A verbal split is not automatically void — the writing requirement of IC 32-21-1-10 applies to owner-signed commission contracts, not agreements between firms — but a verbal agreement is difficult to prove and enforce when the terms are disputed.
